result:
Sometimes I wonder what it would feel like to be a cloud—no, not the poetic fluff of bedtime stories, but the real, violent cumulonimbus that climbs like a skyscraper made of shadow and static. I imagine the first milliseconds of birth: a subtle moistening, a droplet small enough to fit on the tongue of an ant, lifting without hesitation into the open palm of a thermal. Updrafts braid themselves around the infant cloud and haul it sheer miles above the kitchen-table world of birds and wind chimes. The higher it goes, the less it weighs, a marvelous betrayal of everything I learned at the breakfast table: the more you carry, the more you are.  

Inside the belly, liquid shivers, freezes, refreezes into feathered skeletons we call graupel. Electricity stacks in layers like unsent love letters. Helium-blue veins of ice thread toward a heart that doesn’t beat so much as generate pressure, as if the whole storm were one enormous barometer dreaming about ecstatic failure. Somewhere beneath it, human plans—picnic potatoes, football tryouts, a grandmother’s linen—scatter like dryer lint in reverse, unraveling into the merciful dark.

By the time the anvil top mushrooms across the jet stream, every particle of the cloud has entered a debate: precipitate or abstain? A solitary decision but not a lonely one. Arguments conducted in microcharges and centrifugal waltz. When consensus finally flickers, the cloud does something only angels and civil wars do: it tears itself open. First comes the hush, as if someone coughed during prayer, then the sound of fifteen lakes returning to earth through a single keyhole. I try to picture the impossible volume of that applause, the way it flattens cornfields, rehearses riverbeds, stitches every green blade to the ground with liquid commas.

Then—collapse. Towers drip into mist. Gifts spent, the mother cloud unhooks her limbs of lightning and crumbles. The sky seals shut like an envelope licked by sunset. Somewhere in the drifting remnants, perhaps a single ice crystal drifts down slower than any snowflake has a right to. It lands on my unshaven cheek seven hours after the storm has vanished from radar, still cold, barely self. I ask it for its name, but it only glints once, already turning into the puddle in my palm—my own miniature restoration project.
